title: Dog Eyelashes Growing Inward: Causes, Risks, and Veterinary Care
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's eyelashes are rubbing their eye, the risks of corneal ulcers, and why this medium-urgency condition requires a vet visit.
content_html:
TL;DR: Eyelashes growing toward a dog's eye cause severe pain and potential vision loss, requiring a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours to prevent corneal ulcers. Conditions like Distichiasis and Ectopic Cilia act as constant irritants that need professional intervention.

What are the different conditions that cause a dog's eyelashes to grow toward their eye?

When a dog's eyelashes grow toward the eye, it is typically caused by one of three conditions: Distichiasis, Ectopic Cilia, or Trichiasis. Distichiasis occurs when extra lashes grow from the oil glands along the eyelid margin. Ectopic Cilia involves hairs poking through the underside of the eyelid, while Trichiasis involves facial hair rubbing against the eye. In all cases, these hairs act like tiny needles, causing constant irritation and scratching the cornea.

Is it an emergency if my dog has eyelashes rubbing against their eye?

  • This is a Medium Urgency condition that is extremely painful and poses a high risk for corneal ulceration or secondary infections.
  • Seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours to prevent permanent vision damage.
  • Watch for signs such as squinting, pawing at the eye, significant redness, or cloudiness.

Why is taking a photo helpful for my dog's eye triage?

  • High-resolution images allow the vet to see the position of stray hairs and the condition of the cornea.
  • Clear photos help the team determine how quickly your pet needs to be seen.
  • Visuals can help determine if a specialized ophthalmology referral is necessary.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Anomalies of the cilia, such as distichiasis, are common and likely inherited in some dog breeds. Distichiasis is typically only treated if corneal and/or conjunctival disease results. Treatment involves excising, cauterizing, or cryotherapy of the cilia follicles to destroy the follicular base of the eyelids without injuring the eyelid margin. Cryotherapy is commonly used, applied at the base of the cilia beneath the palpebral conjunctiva, although depigmentation of the eyelid margin may occur temporarily. Inadequate cryotherapy can lead to recurrence of distichia.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 489)

faqs.question: Can I pluck the inward-growing eyelash at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to pluck the hairs yourself. Plucking is temporary, painful, and often leaves behind a sharp, short stubble that can cause even more damage to the eye's surface.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of eye irritation from lashes?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include excessive blinking or squinting, watery or mucoid discharge, redness of the whites of the eyes, and your dog rubbing their face against furniture or using their paws.

faqs.question: How are inward-growing eyelashes permanently treated?
faqs.answer: Vets typically use procedures like cryotherapy (freezing the follicles) or electrolysis to permanently destroy the hair follicle, preventing the lash from growing back.
